bio.education: Burke Mountain Academy Dartmouth College
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Specialty: Women's Alpine Skiing (Giant Slalom and Slalom)
  • Graduated from Dartmouth College with a degree in economics
  • U.S. National Champion across multiple disciplines
  • Advocate for Voice in Sport, a community elevating women's voices in athletics
Biographical Information

Growing up in a skiing family, Nina O'Brien recalls weekend trips where her parents would load four children and two dogs into the car for the 3.5-hour drive to the mountains. At age 13, O'Brien moved cross-country to attend Burke Mountain Academy in Vermont, a prestigious ski racing academy where she graduated a year behind future teammate Mikaela Shiffrin. The transition from West Coast to East Coast skiing significantly increased her training volume and allowed her to join the National Training Group at age 15.

O'Brien made her world cup debut at Killington just days before her 19th birthday in November 2016. She quickly progressed through the ranks, earning her spot on the Stifel U.S. Alpine Ski Team that same year. In 2019, O'Brien captured the NorAm overall title, finishing first in slalom, giant slalom and super-G. Her success continued with multiple U.S. national championship titles across various disciplines. 

O'Brien's Olympic debut at the Olympic Winter Games Beijing 2022 showcased both her potential and the unpredictable nature of alpine racing. After posting the sixth-fastest time in the first run of the giant slalom, O'Brien crashed near the finish line during her second run, suffering a compound fracture of her tibia and fibula. The devastating injury required four surgeries and extensive rehabilitation. Demonstrating remarkable mental fortitude, O'Brien returned to competition just nine months later at Killington in November 2022. Her comeback culminated in gold at the 2023 world championships in Courchevel-Meribel, France, where she teamed with Paula Moltzan, Tommy Ford and River Radamus to win the mixed team parallel event.

Adversity struck again in September 2023 when O'Brien broke the same leg during a training camp in New Zealand. The second fracture required another complete rehabilitation process, causing her to miss the entire 2023-24 season. Through both recovery periods, O'Brien leaned on the support of teammates, coaches and especially Mikaela Shiffrin, who provided encouragement during the most difficult moments. O'Brien returned to world cup competition in October 2024, finishing seventh at Sölden and sixth at Killington—both career-best results that demonstrated her technical skills remained sharper than ever.

Off the slopes, O'Brien graduated from Dartmouth College with a degree in economics, alongside her younger brother Preston. She has worked in finance for a private equity firm in Denver and remains actively involved with Voice in Sport, an organization dedicated to elevating women's voices in athletics.

Olympic Experience
  • 2-time Olympian
    • Olympic Games Milan 2026, (Slalom - Women, Team Alpine Combined - Women), 20th (Giant Slalom - Women)
    • Olympic Games Beijing 2022, (Giant Slalom - Women)
World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2025 – 4th (Parallel Team - Mixed), 19th (Giant Slalom - Women)
  • Years of participation: Giant Slalom - Women 2019, 2021, 2023, 2025; Parallel Team - Mixed 2025; Parallel - Women 2021, 2023; Slalom - Women 2019, 2021, 2023; Team - Mixed 2021, 2023
  • Medals: 1 (1 gold)
    • Gold – 2023 (Team - Mixed)

bio.education: Picabo Street Academy (Park City)
bio.fun_fact: Hobbies include acrylic painting, rock climbing, sled hockey and connecting with the broader adaptive sports community
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Specialty: Para Alpine Skiing (Sitting) — LW12-1 Classification
  • Born with Spina Bifida; began training with the National Ability Center High Performance Alpine Team in Park City, Utah at age 13 in 2017
  • U.S. Ski & Snowboard Adaptive Athlete of the Year, 2024–25 season
  • Sisters in Sports athlete
  • Hobbies include acrylic painting, rock climbing, sled hockey and connecting with the broader adaptive sports community
Biographical Information

Saylor O'Brien's relationship with skiing began at age four, growing up in Woodland, Utah, with the Wasatch Range as her backyard. Born with Spina Bifida — a congenital neural tube defect in which the spinal cord does not fully develop, resulting in varying degrees of paralysis and loss of sensation below the affected vertebrae — she has navigated the world from a sit-ski since childhood. The sport arrived early, the commitment arrived later, and the crystallizing moment came when she was 10 years old: watching Paralympic ski racing for the first time and recognizing, with the clarity that sometimes only a child has, that this was exactly what she was meant to do.

In 2017, at age 13, O'Brien began training with the National Ability Center's High Performance Alpine Team in Park City, Utah — one of the most rigorous adaptive ski development programs in the country. She also attended the Picabo Street Academy, a ski-focused school in Park City named for Olympic gold medalist Picabo Street, and graduated in 2020. The environment placed her within a community of elite competitive skiers from an early age, and her technical development reflected it.

She joined the U.S. Para Alpine Ski Team in 2022. The following year, at the 2023 FIS Alpine Skiing World Championships in Veysonnaz, Switzerland, O'Brien earned silver and bronze medals in slalom and giant slalom d in the LW12-1 women's sitting division — two podium finishes at her first world championships, announcing her arrival on the international stage with unmistakable force. She has continued to build, claiming more world cup podiums and U.S. national championship titles across her career and winning the U.S. Ski & Snowboard Adaptive Athlete of the Year award for the 2024–25 season. The 2024–25 season also brought a first world cup victory, in downhill, along with three additional international podiums.

Away from racing, O'Brien serves as a Sisters in Sports athlete — a program that connects women athletes with disabilities to one another, to resources and to the confidence that sustained athletic engagement builds. She is deliberate about using her platform to challenge the assumptions made about what people with disabilities can achieve and to demonstrate through action, not argument, that those assumptions are wrong.

World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2023
  • Years of Participation: 2023
  • Medals: 2 (bronze)
  • Bronze - 2023 (super-G, alpine combined)

bio.education: Stanford University
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Daughter of Dan and Karen O’Hara
  • Has one brother, Jerry, and one sister, Erin
  • Father, Dan, is a former fighter pilot in the U.S. Navy and brother, Jerry, is also a Navy pilot
  • A lifelong forward, switched to the left back position during the 2012 Olympic qualifying tournament and went on to play every minute at outside back for Team USA during the 2012 Olympic Games
  • Selected third overall by FC Gold Pride in the 2010 WPS Draft
  • Won the 2009 Hermann Trophy as the top collegiate soccer player in the country
  • Interests include surfing and baking, with her mother, Karen, providing all her baking recipes
  • Host of the Just Women’s Sports podcast where her goal is to generate “open, candid conversations” about the lives of athletes, particularly female athletes”

bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Daughter of Daniel and Jennifer O'Neil
  • Has one sister, Lindsay
  • Bridget began diving at the age of six
  • She trains three to four hours a day, six days a week
  • Competed in her first junior nationals in 2010 and her first senior nationals in 2014
  • Bridget's favorite dive is 405B on 3m
  • She enjoys talking with friends in between dives at competitions
  • She loves having team dinners before competition because it reminds her to appreciate the moments with her teammates and takes the pressure off
  • Her favorite music to listen to is country, but she doesn't usually listen to music at meets
  • Enjoys spending time with friends and family, cooking, the outdoors, and being involved in her church
  • Her great uncle, John Thomas O'Neil, was a 2x Olympic gold medalist in rugby for Team USA in 1920 and 1924 Olympics
  • Describes herself as driven, passionate and faithful
  • Favorite movie is Paul Blart Mall Cop
Career Highlights
  • Two-time World Junior Championships team member (2016, 2018)

  • Hobbies include hiking, reading science fiction novels, building with Legos, and home improvement projects
Biographical Information

Danny O'Shea's Olympic dream began at a young age when he watched the Winter Games on television. He made his senior U.S. championships debut in 2013 with Tarah Kayne, and together they captured the 2016 U.S. national title and the 2018 Four Continents Championship. After nearly nine years together, the partnership ended in December 2020, and O'Shea retired from competitive skating.

O'Shea briefly returned to competition in 2021 with Chelsea Liu, but the partnership ended after a dangerous fall from a lift in November 2021. He retired again, working in real estate and helping Drew Meekins as a coaching assistant. In 2022, when young pairs skater Ellie Kam needed a practice partner after her partnership ended, O'Shea stepped in. The chemistry was immediate, and they officially teamed up in September 2022. The partnership was unprecedented, with the largest age difference, of nearly 14 years, for any U.S. pair to compete at World Figure Skating Championships since at least 1990.

In their debut season, they won bronze at the 2023 U.S. Figure Skating Championships and finished 12th at the 2023 world championships, capturing their first national title at the 2024 U.S. Figure Skating Championships. The 2024-25 season brought breakthrough performances: they won silver at Skate America with 201.73 points—O'Shea's first time breaking 200 points with any partner—and qualified for the Grand Prix Final, becoming only the fourth U.S. team to do so in the last 14 editions. 

Kam and O'Shea were named to the 2026 Olympic team. At age 35, O'Shea became the oldest U.S. Olympic pairs skater since 1932 and the oldest figure skater from any country to make an Olympic debut since 1948. Beyond skating, O'Shea works as a real estate agent and coaches young pair skaters, giving back to the sport that has defined his life for three decades.

World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2025 – 7th (Pairs - Mixed)
  • Years of participation: Pairs - Mixed 2016, 2023, 2024, 2025
  • Top finish: 7th – 2025 (Pairs - Mixed)
Olympic Experience
  • 1-time Olympian; 1-time Olympic medalist (1 gold)
    • Olympic Games Milan 2026, gold (Team - Mixed), 9th (Pairs - Mixed)
